1: Hear, O heads of Jacob and rulers of the house of Israel — shim'u-na ro'shei Ya'aqov u-qetsine beit Yisra'el: the second major summons in the book, now directed specifically at the civil leadership. Ha-lo lakhem lada'at et-ha-mishpat (is it not for you to know justice?) — mishpat (justice, proper judgment) is precisely their assigned role; they are indicted for failing at their own job description. The rhetorical question assumes a 'yes' answer: of course they know mishpat — they are the ones charged with administering it.
2: You who hate what is good and love what is evil — sone'ei tov ve-'ohavei ra': the emotional inversion; the moral vocabulary of love/hate applied to ethical categories (cf. Amos 5:15). Gorze'ei 'oram me'aleihem (who tear the skin from my people) — gazar (to cut, tear) applied to skin-tearing; the metaphor of cannibalism begins with this gesture. The leaders' economic exploitation is rendered as literal devouring of the people's bodies.
3: Who eat the flesh of my people and strip their skin — the cannibalism metaphor intensifies across three parallel lines: 'asher akhal she'er ammi (who eat the flesh of my people), ve-'orem me'aleihem hipshitu (strip their skin from them), ve-et-atsmotam pitstseihu (break their bones in pieces), u-farshu ka'asher bassir (chop them up like meat in a pot). The cooking-pot image (siyar / qallachat) is precise: the poor are butchered and cooked by the rich the way animals are processed for food. This is the most graphic economic-injustice metaphor in the OT.
4: Then they will cry out to the LORD, but he will not answer them — az yiz'aqu el-YHWH ve-lo ya'aneh otam: the covenant reversal: in distress the wicked cry to YHWH, but YHWH's face is hidden. Yastir panav meihem (he will hide his face from them) — divine face-hiding (hester panim) is the removal of covenant presence and protection (Deut 31:17-18; Ps 30:7; Isa 54:8). Ka'asher hera'u ma'allehem (because their deeds have been evil) — the hiding is proportional to their treatment of the poor.
5: The prophets who lead my people astray — ha-nevi'im ha-mathi'im et-ammi: false prophets operate on an economic basis: nashakh be-shineihem (who cry 'Peace' — lit. 'bite with their teeth') when they have something to eat — the biting/eating suggests they receive payment in kind. Ve-qiddeshupilchamah (but declare war against the one who gives them nothing to eat) — for those who do not feed them, the oracle changes to judgment. The prophetic word is for sale; the content determined by the client's payment.
6: Therefore it shall be night for you, with no vision — lachen layil lakhem me-chazyon: divine judgment on false prophets takes the form of prophetic darkness — no vision (chazyon), no divination (qesem). Va'avah hashemesh al-ha-nevi'im (the sun shall set over the prophets) — losing the prophetic light/sun is the withdrawal of the capacity for divine communication. Va'achshakh aleihem ha-yom (and the day shall go dark over them) — the sun that illuminated genuine vision goes out; the false prophets lose even their false light.
7: The seers shall be ashamed, and the diviners put to shame — ve-voshu ha-chozim ve-chapher ha-qosmim: chozeh (seer, visionary) and qosem (diviner — using lots, entrails, or other means) — both categories of professional oracle-givers are silenced. Ve-'atfu kol-levat sefam (they shall all cover their mouths) — the mouth-covering gesture was used for uncleanness (Lev 13:45; Ezek 24:17) and shame; the professional prophets cover their mouths because there is nothing left to say: ein ma'aneh Elohim (there is no answer from God).
8: But as for me, I am filled with power — ve-ulam anokhi male'ti khoach: the emphatic contrast (ve-ulam) distinguishes Micah from the false prophets. His threefold equipment: ruach YHWH (Spirit of the LORD), mishpat (justice), and gevurah (strength/might). This is Micah's prophetic self-authorization against the opposition in vv6-7. Le-haggid le-Ya'aqov pish'o (to declare to Jacob his transgression) — the content of his prophetic task is specifically the announcement of pesha' (transgression), not comfortable oracles.
9: Hear this, you heads of the house of Jacob — the third summons in the book; the same leadership addressed in 3:1. Ha-mete'avim mishpat (who despise justice) — ta'av (abhor, despise utterly) for the very thing they are supposed to administer. Ve-et kol-ha-yesharah ye'aqqeshu (and make crooked all that is straight) — yaqqash/aqash (make crooked, twist) applied to what is yesharah (straight, right). The leaders geometrically distort the moral-legal landscape.
10: Who build Zion with blood and Jerusalem with wrongdoing — boneh Tsiyon be-damim u-Yerushalam be-'avlah: the capital's physical construction is financed by damim (blood, bloodshed — plural) and 'avlah (wrongdoing, injustice). The magnificent temple-city is built on a foundation of violence and injustice — a structural indictment: the building materials are the proceeds of oppression.
11: Her rulers judge for a bribe; her priests teach for payment; her prophets give oracles for money — the three institutions that should maintain covenant life are all corrupt: ro'sheha be-shochad yishpotu (rulers judge for bribes), kohaneiha be-mechir yoruhu (priests teach for payment), nevi'eha be-keseph yiqsemu (prophets divine for money). The three-fold institutional corruption covers the totality of covenant leadership. Their self-assurance follows: halo YHWH be-qirbeinu (is not the LORD among us?) — false confidence in divine presence despite corrupt practices. Lo-tavo' aleinu ra'ah (disaster will not come upon us) — the fatal presumption that covenant status provides immunity from judgment.
12: Therefore because of you Zion shall be plowed as a field — lachen biglalekhem Tsiyon sadeh techaresh: the most famous verse in Micah and one of the most remarkable in the OT, cited verbatim in Jer 26:18 as the text that saved Jeremiah's life: the elders argued that Micah had prophesied this destruction under Hezekiah, and Hezekiah did not put him to death — establishing the precedent that prophets of doom should not be killed. Ve-Yerushalam 'iyyim tihyeh (Jerusalem shall become a heap of ruins) and ve-har ha-bayit le-vamot ya'ar (and the mountain of the temple a wooded height) — complete deforestation and depopulation of the temple mount.
